primer.markdown:
# Fill in the seven variables. Pick a pattern. Ship a plan.

You have read the universal template, the two-agent pattern, the MCP tool layer, and the five-step pipeline. Now it is your turn. This is the canonical last module of the Quest: you walk away with a plan-repository for your *own* design-automation tool, shaped by the exact same contract CANVA-CODE used when it scored 9.18/10.

Start by choosing values for the seven template variables (from Module 01). Your `PROJECT_NAME` (for example `FigmaAI` or `SketchAssist`). Your `PRIMARY_API` (Figma, Adobe Express, Canva, Sketch — whichever you actually want to build against). Your `SECONDARY_SERVICES` (DALL-E 3, Midjourney, NanoBanana, Stable Diffusion). Your `TARGET_USERS`, `SPEED_TARGET`, `QUALITY_TARGET`, and `ARCHITECTURE_PATTERN` (use the decision matrix from Module 02 — if you will make 3-10 API calls per request, choose `two-agent`).

Then hand those seven values to the pipeline from Module 04. The CANVA-CODE retrospective (DESIGN-TOOL-PLANNER/README.md lines 1-10) is the shape your output will take: a one-line project tagline, a natural-language usage example, a quick-start, and links to the six documents (`PROJECT-OVERVIEW`, `ARCHITECTURE`, `API-INTEGRATION`, `WORKFLOWS`, `README`, `PROJECT-SUMMARY`). In Phase 1 of LUXOR Academy you queue your seven variables in the form below; **150 XP vests the moment you submit**. In Phase 3 the pipeline runs live and returns a permalink to your generated plan repo. Future learners reading this Quest will see *your* tool alongside CANVA-CODE in the examples gallery. That is the sovereignty clause — you own the plan the Academy generated, attribution and all.
primer.markdown_es:
# Completa las siete variables. Elige un patrón. Lanza un plan.

Has leído la plantilla universal, el patrón de dos agentes, la capa de herramientas MCP y el pipeline de cinco pasos. Ahora te toca a ti. Éste es el módulo canónico final del Quest: te vas con un repositorio de plan para *tu propia* herramienta de automatización de diseño, moldeado por el mismo contrato que CANVA-CODE usó cuando obtuvo 9.18/10.

Empieza eligiendo valores para las siete variables de la plantilla (del Módulo 01). Tu `PROJECT_NAME` (por ejemplo `FigmaAI` o `SketchAssist`). Tu `PRIMARY_API` (Figma, Adobe Express, Canva, Sketch). Tus `SECONDARY_SERVICES` (DALL-E 3, Midjourney, NanoBanana, Stable Diffusion). Tus `TARGET_USERS`, `SPEED_TARGET`, `QUALITY_TARGET` y `ARCHITECTURE_PATTERN` (usa la matriz de decisión del Módulo 02 — si harás de 3 a 10 llamadas por petición, elige `two-agent`).

Luego entrega esas siete variables al pipeline del Módulo 04. En la Fase 1 de LUXOR Academy cargas las variables en el formulario; **150 XP se acreditan al enviar**. En la Fase 3 el pipeline corre en vivo y devuelve un permalink al repositorio de plan generado. Los futuros aprendices verán *tu* herramienta junto a CANVA-CODE. Esa es la cláusula de soberanía — eres dueño del plan que la Academia generó, con tu atribución.
primer.key_insight: The Quest ends with your repo, not a badge. Seven variables in; a plan repo out. Future learners browse your project in the examples gallery, permalinked to your name.
primer.key_insight_es: El Quest termina con tu repositorio, no con una insignia. Siete variables entran; un repositorio de plan sale. Futuros aprendices verán tu proyecto con tu atribución.

visual.alt: A flow diagram: on the left, a seven-slot input form lists PROJECT_NAME, PRIMARY_API, SECONDARY_SERVICES, TARGET_USERS, SPEED_TARGET, QUALITY_TARGET, ARCHITECTURE_PATTERN with example fills (FigmaAI, Figma REST API, DALL-E 3, product designers, < 30s, 90%+, two-agent). A coral arrow flows into a pipeline gear labeled 'analyze → research → architect → plan → document'. On the right, a stack of six document ribbons (PROJECT-OVERVIEW, ARCHITECTURE, API-INTEGRATION, WORKFLOWS, README, PROJECT-SUMMARY) emerges, bound by a permalink tag stamped with the learner's handle.

sandbox_policy.csp: default-src 'none'; style-src 'unsafe-inline' https://fonts.googleapis.com; script-src 'unsafe-inline'; img-src data:; font-src https://fonts.gstatic.com; connect-src 'none'; frame-ancestors 'self'

interactive.success_criterion: Learner fills in the seven template variables (PROJECT_NAME, PRIMARY_API, SECONDARY_SERVICES, TARGET_USERS, SPEED_TARGET, QUALITY_TARGET, ARCHITECTURE_PATTERN), reviews the preview of what the five-step pipeline would generate (six-document table of contents), and submits. Phase 1 stores the submission locally for Phase 3 pipeline activation; the submit button is a visual stub in Phase 1 that logs to localStorage under `luxor.academy.shipped.design-tool-planner`.

artifact.excerpt:
# 🎨 CANVA-CODE

**AI-Powered Design Automation | Next-Generation Design Tool**

Transform design workflows with natural language. Create professional designs in < 60 seconds.

```
Designer: "Create an Instagram post for our product launch"
CANVA-CODE: ✨ [Professional design ready in 28 seconds]
```

---

## 🚀 What is CANVA-CODE?

CANVA-CODE is an **AI-powered design automation platform** that combines:
- **[Claude Agent SDK](https://docs.claude.com/en/docs/agent-sdk)**: Intelligent orchestration
- **[Canva Connect API](https://www.canva.dev/docs/connect/)**: Professional design infrastructure

questions.prompt: You are planning a design-automation tool that will make about 5 API calls per generation request (parse intent, fetch template, generate one image, assemble design, export). Per the decision matrix in CLAUDE-SDK-AGENT-BUILDER-GUIDE.md lines 17-31, which ARCHITECTURE_PATTERN should you set?
questions.explanation: The decision matrix (lines 17-26) and the decision flowchart (lines 730-751) both map 3-10 API calls to `two-agent`. Your case (5 calls) sits squarely in that band, and two-agent carries the only validated quality number (9.5/10 on CANVA-CODE).

questions.prompt: The CANVA-CODE README.md opens with a one-line tagline and an in/out example before any prose. What is the purpose of that shape (lines 1-10) for the plan repositories this Quest produces?
options: To demonstrate the natural-language-in / artifact-out contract at a glance, before the reader scrolls
questions.explanation: Lines 7-10 of README.md show the shape: `Designer: "Create an Instagram post..."` → `CANVA-CODE: ✨ [Professional design ready in 28 seconds]`. That in/out example compresses the entire value proposition into four lines and is the shape every plan-repo produced by this Quest should open with.

questions.prompt: Why is this the canonical last module of the Quest (rather than a summary quiz)?
options: Sovereignty — you leave holding a plan repo of your own, not a score
Because Quests require an even number of modules
Because the validator refuses to approve summary quizzes
questions.explanation: Per the MERCURIO canonical-last-module pattern used across LUXOR Academy, the final module must hand the learner a permalink to their own artifact — a plan repo, a deployed app, a teachable specimen — not a badge. The Quest trains for the next repo, not for itself.
